skill.name: spec-discovery
skill.description: Parse specification files to extract requirement IDs, titles, source paths, content hashes, and traceability metadata.
skill.summary: Build traceable requirement manifests from repository specification files.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static detections for Ruby or shell backtick execution are false positives caused by Markdown inline code and a JSON example in SKILL.md. The weak cryptographic algorithm detections are also false positives; the skill references SHA-256 hashing, not a weak algorithm. The remaining concern is intentional filesystem traversal of spec directories and output of absolute repository roots, which merits a publishing warning but does not indicate malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Repository Path and File Metadata Exposure
medium_findings.description: The workflow instructs agents to load repository roots, walk spec directories, compute file hashes, and include source root paths in the manifest. This is legitimate for traceability, but it can expose local directory structure and file metadata if users run it on sensitive repositories or share the output externally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is explicit in the workflow and output schema. The risk is moderate because it supports the stated purpose and does not show exfiltration or hidden network transmission.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Execution False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution at multiple SKILL.md lines. These locations are Markdown inline code spans or fenced JSON example content, not executable Ruby or shell code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md contains prose and Markdown examples only. No executable script, Ruby file, shell command runner, or command substitution context is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns at SKILL.md lines 3 and 12. No weak cryptographic algorithm is present; the skill describes traceability hashes and later specifies SHA-256.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain metadata and a table header, not cryptographic implementation. The only concrete algorithm named in the file is SHA-256, which is appropriate for file integrity metadata.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Traversal for Spec Discovery
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill asks agents to scan spec directories across a primary repository and optional extra repositories. Users should scope repository roots carefully before producing shareable manifests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The traversal is directly instructed, but it is restricted to specification paths by the skill description. Risk depends on user-selected repository roots and where the manifest is shared.

content.value_statement: Teams lose traceability when requirements are spread across repositories. This skill finds requirement headings and builds a manifest with paths, hashes, and duplicate notes.
actual_capabilities: Scans a primary repository root and optional extra repository roots for specification files.
Finds requirement headings that use REQ-style identifiers.
Extracts requirement IDs, titles, relative paths, links, and source repository names.
Computes SHA-256 file hashes for traceability records.
Flags duplicate requirement IDs and files with no detected IDs.
Can emit a JSON or TOON manifest for downstream agent workflows.
limitations: It depends on requirement headings that follow the documented REQ identifier pattern.
It does not validate requirement quality, completeness, or business priority.
It can expose local repository paths if manifest output is shared without review.
It reports an empty result when no specs directory exists.

use_cases.title: Trace Requirements Before Implementation
use_cases.description: Generate a manifest of requirement IDs before engineers start coding against a specification set.

use_cases.title: Audit Duplicate Spec IDs
use_cases.description: Find duplicate requirement identifiers across repositories and record them for cleanup.

use_cases.title: Prepare Agent Context
use_cases.description: Create compact traceability data for agents that need reliable links back to source specs.

prompt_templates.title: Find Specs in One Repository
prompt_templates.prompt: Use spec-discovery on the current repository. Return a concise manifest of requirement IDs, titles, relative paths, and hashes.

prompt_templates.title: Include External Requests
prompt_templates.prompt: Run spec-discovery with external requests included. Flag files with no detected IDs and note any duplicate requirement IDs.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Repositories
prompt_templates.prompt: Use spec-discovery across the main repository and the provided extra repositories. Sort results by repository, path, and requirement ID.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Compact Agent Context
prompt_templates.prompt: Run spec-discovery and produce TOON output for agent context. Preserve ID casing and include duplicate notes where relevant.

output_examples.input: Scan the current repository for requirement specs.
output_examples.output: A manifest listing each requirement ID, title, source repository, relative file path, link anchor, and file hash.

output_examples.input: Find duplicate requirement IDs across the main repository and two extra repositories.
output: A sorted requirements manifest grouped by repository.
Duplicate IDs are called out in notes for follow-up review.
Files without recognized requirement headings are listed as verification items.

output_examples.input: Create compact context for a downstream implementation agent.
output_examples.output: A TOON-style manifest with requirement identifiers, titles, links, and traceability metadata suitable for prompt embedding.

best_practices: Review manifest output before sharing it outside the repository team.
Keep requirement headings consistent with the documented REQ identifier pattern.
Use relative paths in downstream reports when absolute local paths are not required.
anti_patterns: Do not run the skill against unrelated repository roots that contain sensitive files.
Do not treat missing IDs as proof that a spec file has no requirements.
Do not publish manifests with local absolute paths unless that metadata is approved.

faq.question: What files does this skill look for?
faq.answer: It focuses on specification directories, including external request specs when that option is enabled.

faq.question: What requirement format does it detect?
faq.answer: It detects headings that begin with REQ-style identifiers followed by an optional title.

faq.question: Does it modify repository files?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow reads specification files and emits a manifest.

faq.question: Why does it compute hashes?
faq.answer: Hashes help downstream tools confirm which source file version produced each traceability record.

faq.question: Can it scan more than one repository?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports a primary root and optional extra repository roots.

faq.question: What should users review before sharing output?
faq.answer: Users should review repository names, paths, links, and any absolute root paths in the manifest.
